Impuesto Parcelario 2026

La Mesa Directiva adoptó las Resoluciones 7-9 autorizando reducciones de personal certificado y clasificado y reasignación de administradores para el próximo año — el proceso anual de balance de presupuesto. Se aprobó un acuerdo con TeamCivX y Godbe Research para estudiar la viabilidad del impuesto parcelario, dando inicio al esfuerzo de varios años que eventualmente produciría la Medida E. Se aprobaron informes de antigüedad de personal certificado y clasificado.

5 puntos de agenda
7.102:41:31 Presentación Discusión Comentario público Voto (5-0)Approval of Agreement between the Redwood City School District and TeamCivX and Godbe Research to conduct a Parcel Tax Measure Feasibility StudyAction
Aprobación del acuerdo entre el Distrito Escolar de Redwood City y TeamCivX y Godbe Research para realizar un estudio de viabilidad de la medida de impuesto parcelario
Comentarios públicos (1):
Chris Rebel (2m 17s) — Urged the board to be transparent about prioritization between parcel taxes and future bonds, warning that community members have limited funds and there are many competing tax measures on the November ballot.
TeamCivX_RedwoodCitySD1_Contract_March2024.pdf (176 KB)Godbe RCSD PT Survey Agreement 3.1.24 Signed.pdf (457 KB)
7.200:03:41 Presentación Discusión Comentario público Voto (5-0)Adoption of Resolution No. 7, Reduction or Elimination of Certificated Services for Fiscal Year 2023-2024Action
Adopción de la Resolución No. 7, Reducción o eliminación de servicios certificados para el año fiscal 2023-2024
Comentarios públicos (17):
Esther Chyan (2m 2s) — Expressed concern about reading level cuts and urged the board and trustees to find creative, sustainable solutions to support students and families.
Tom Gierke (1m 34s) — Voiced concern about proposed cuts to music programs, citing their developmental benefits for students and arguing the cuts demonstrate poor planning of Prop 28 funds.
Hana Ma (2m 14s) — Asked about alternatives explored before staffing cuts, questioned the distribution of cuts across schools, and urged creative revenue-generating approaches.
Lauren Abrahams (2m 14s) — Spoke as the mental health counselor at Taft, emphasizing the unique and high-need demographics of Taft students and urging the board to reconsider reducing her position to 0.5 FTE.
Meghan O'Reilly-Green (1m 56s) — Expressed appreciation for the board's difficult work and advocated for a larger parcel tax, comparing RCSD's current $85 parcel tax unfavorably to neighboring districts.
Mary Ann Nakhla (1m 1s) — Urged the board to reconsider cutting mental health liaison positions, emphasizing the deep relationships liaisons have built with students across the district.
Lisbeth Estrada (1m 22s) — Expressed deep appreciation to Dr. Baker for prioritizing equity and supporting the Hoover community, including special education students.
Saoirse Rafferty (1m 9s) — A fourth-grade student at Orion who expressed love for her school and teachers and hoped a larger parcel tax could be passed so all kids have what they need.
Sue Jorgensen (1m 31s) — Taft librarian who criticized inadequate communication from the district about librarian layoffs, expressed distrust, and declined to support a parcel tax at this time.
Ann Berljafa (3m 16s) — Advocated for enshrining a 25-to-1 class size ratio for K-2 classrooms in the collective bargaining agreement, warning that larger classes cause students to fall through the cracks.
Shari Tsuchiyama (2m 13s) — First-grade teacher at Roosevelt who shared personal experience of the benefits of smaller class sizes and urged the board to permanently cap TK-2nd grade at 25 students.
Jessica McLain (2m 18s) — Education specialist who raised safety and quality concerns about increasing caseloads in moderate-to-severe special education classrooms, calling for an additional ESLC and SLC class.
Alison Sudol (1m 47s) — Teacher of an extensive support needs classroom who described increasing unsafe behaviors correlated with rising class sizes and proposed opening one additional ESN and SLC classroom.
Erinn Washburn (2m 27s) — Music teacher who outlined the academic, social-emotional, and developmental benefits of well-funded music education programs and urged administrators and parents to prioritize them.
Dianne Gutierrez (3m 5s) — School counselor at Orion who shared a story about a dysregulated student to illustrate the importance of having a counselor available as a safety net for students.
Lilli Milton (1m 34s) — Orion and Kennedy parent and RCEF board member who urged the board to reject the resolution, seek authentic educator input, and pursue an equitable approach to cuts while rebuilding community trust.
Dave Privasch (1m 14s) — Expressed that cutting librarians, mental health staff, and STEM teachers seems unconscionable given the area's wealth, and echoed calls for creative solutions before approving reductions.
RESOLUTION 7 - Certificated PKS 3.6.24 final .pdf (220 KB)2024-25 Services and Funding.pdf (2,912 KB)

Comentarios públicos (4):
Salvador Gomez Nava (2m 29s) — Spanish-speaking parent of a Taft student who emphasized the critical importance of school-based counseling for children dealing with trauma and urged the board to maintain those services.
Ana Solorio (1m 20s) — President of CSEA Chapter 5 who opposed the layoff of any classified employees and requested the board not approve the resolution.
Connie Hamilton (2m 59s) — LMA librarian at Clifford School who questioned the equity of laying off three experienced, lower-paid librarians while planning to hire new LMTA staff at higher pay, and expressed devastation at losing her position.
Beverly Aguilar (3m 5s) — LMA librarian at Garfield School who, together with Connie Hamilton, asked the board not to eliminate their positions, citing 30 combined years of experience, bilingual skills, and the reclassification process already underway.

Impuesto Parcelario 2026

La junta discutió un posible impuesto sobre parcelas para la papeleta de noviembre de 2024, revisó reportes financieros intermedios e informes de auditoría de escuelas charter incluyendo Rocketship Redwood City, Connect Community Charter, y KIPP Excelencia Community Prep, y recibió una actualización anual del Plan de Responsabilidad y Control Local (LCAP).

13 puntos de agenda
800:02:16 Comentario públicoOral Communication
Comentarios públicos (1):
Elizabeth Flagle (3m 34s) — A fourth-grade parent at Adelante Selby voiced concern about potential budget cuts including consolidating three fourth-grade classrooms into two, eliminating a full-time teacher position, and reducing yard duty and specialist support, urging the board to prioritize maintaining smaller class sizes for vulnerable students.
900:07:39 Presentación Discusión Comentario públicoDiscussion Item(s)
Comentarios públicos (3):
Jessica Shade (1m 41s) — A parent of two students at Orion expressed strong support for a parcel tax to replace expiring COVID stimulus funds and to maintain mental health counselors, reading specialists, class aides, ESL support, STEAM instructors, and class-size reduction.
Brandon Matthews (25s) — A homeowner and parent of a kindergartner in Redwood City voiced support for moving as quickly as possible toward a parcel tax to support the schools.
Elizabeth Flagle (1m 28s) — A parent and former PTO board member at Adelante Selby expressed support for the parcel tax feasibility study while urging the board to learn from the lessons of Measure H's narrow defeat and to look at strategies to counter online opposition, and also noted a technical barrier preventing a fellow parent from submitting a speaker card.

La Junta sostuvo sesiones cerradas para negociar el precio y términos de un acuerdo de intercambio de propiedad para 750 Bradford St con The Sobrato Organization y para evaluar el desempeño del Superintendente. En sesión abierta, la Junta aprobó las Tarjetas de Responsabilidad Escolar 2023, aceptó propuestas laborales de la Asociación de Maestros de Redwood City y de la Asociación de Empleados de Escuelas de California Capítulo V, y adoptó la Resolución No. 6 respecto a posibles reducciones de empleados certificados para el año fiscal 2023-2024.

800:03:34 Comentario públicoOral Communication
Comentarios públicos (2):
Brian Cagle (49s) — As RCTA Negotiations Chair, formally submitted the CTA sunshine proposal opening Articles 4 (working hours), 6 (compensation), and 8 (class size) for negotiations with the district.
Aaron Washburn (56s) — As RCTA President, explained the rationale for opening those articles, emphasizing the need to protect early elementary students from overcrowded classrooms and attract quality educators.

La Junta eligió nuevos directivos para el año 2023-2024, reconoció a Cecilia I. Márquez, discutió el Estudio Financiero de Energía Solar Fase 2 de todo el Distrito y aprobó su lista final de proyectos, y escuchó presentaciones de las escuelas Kennedy y McKinley. La Junta también aprobó varias actualizaciones de políticas incluyendo la Política de la Junta 6174 para Estudiantes de Inglés, la Política de la Junta 5144.1 sobre Suspensión y Expulsión, y la Política de la Junta 6173.1 para Educación de Jóvenes en Cuidado de Crianza, además de la aprobación de compras adicionales del currículo Language Power y personal necesario para el año escolar.

NOV 15 2023
Sesión de Estudio3h 16m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

Discussion on Dyslexia; Approval of the Agenda; Approval of Bond Program Consent Items; Approval of Measure T Bond Program Tracking List of Items Under $20k; Approval of Proposal for the Infrastructure of Two (2) Video/Intercom Buzzers with Atlas Pellizzari for Hawes School; Approval of Proposal for Theatrical Lighting Upgrades with Atlas Pellizzari for McKinley Institute of Technology

18 puntos de agenda
300:01:32 Presentación Discusión Comentario públicoStudy Session 6:00 p.m. (Open to the Public)
Comentarios públicos (4):
Ors Neighbor (3m 29s) — A father described his son Leor's experience being diagnosed with dyslexia privately at great cost after the school determined he did not have a specific learning disability, and urged the community to find ways to teach reading to all children regardless of resources.
Giselle Hale (3m 19s) — A parent of two dyslexic students and a community advocate urged the board to treat dyslexia as a general education issue, highlighted districts making gains by training all teachers in Orton-Gillingham techniques, and emphasized that implementation fidelity matters as much as curriculum selection.
Fallon Scoggins (6m 1s) — A mother of a severely dyslexic and dysgraphic third-grade son described his struggles in school, the inadequacy of pullout services and after-school interventions, the disproportionate impact on Black students, and urged the board to make structured literacy instruction available to all students throughout the school day.
Jennifer Salma (6m 55s) — A regional leader with Decoding Dyslexia California thanked the board for engaging with the California Dyslexia Guidelines, emphasized that dyslexia is a neurological difference with many strengths, called for structured literacy programs to be implemented with fidelity and for principals to support teachers in implementation, and noted that most dyslexic students should be served in general education with tier 1 and tier 2 support.
3.1Discussion on DyslexiaDiscussion
Dyslexia Study Session.pdf (4,366 KB)

NOV 15 2023
Reunión de la Junta3h 16m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda

Approval of the Agenda

2 puntos de agenda
300:01:32 Presentación Discusión Comentario públicoChanges to the Agenda
Comentarios públicos (4):
Ors Neighbor (3m 29s) — A father described his son Leor's experience being diagnosed with dyslexia privately at significant expense after the school determined he did not qualify for special education services despite low reading scores, and urged the district to support all students regardless of financial means.
Giselle Hale (3m 19s) — A parent of two dyslexic students and community advocate urged the board to treat dyslexia as a general education issue, highlighted districts succeeding through Orton-Gillingham training for all teachers, and stressed that implementation fidelity matters more than curriculum choice alone.
Fallon Scoggins (6m 1s) — A mother of a son with severe dyslexia and dysgraphia described the emotional and academic toll on her family, the inaccessibility of after-school interventions for many families, and urgently called for structured literacy instruction to be available throughout the school day for all students.
Jennifer Salma (6m 55s) — A regional leader with Decoding Dyslexia California thanked the district for engaging with the California Dyslexia Guidelines, emphasized dyslexia as a neurological difference with associated strengths rather than a disability, and urged fidelity in program implementation and robust principal and teacher support.
4.1Approval of the AgendaAction
Aprobación de la agenda

NOV 2 2023
Sesión de Estudio1h 58m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda

La junta realizó una sesión de estudio para revisar una actualización sobre iniciativas de Vivienda para la Fuerza Laboral del distrito.

2 puntos de agenda
5.1Update on Workforce Housing
RCSD Study Session Slides1_Workforce Hsg_2023-11-02.pdf (4,155 KB)RCSD Study Session Slides2_JUHSD_Workforce Hsg_2023-11-02.pdf (3,532 KB)RCSD Workforce Housing FAQs 1.0_Nov.2023.pdf (159 KB)
601:40:50 Comentario públicoOral Communication
Comentarios públicos (7):
Chris R. (3m 30s) — Argued that using Prop 39 bond funds for workforce housing violates the California Constitution, questioned whether teacher housing actually serves teachers effectively, and suggested parcel taxes or housing vouchers as better alternatives.
Elizabeth Starks (2m 10s) — Expressed support for teachers but emphasized that children and families — especially in the Spanish-speaking community — need more after-school spaces and resources, and urged the district to focus on hiring dedicated teachers.
Maria Stockton (2m 20s) — Speaking as a classified union leader, she defended the essential role of classified staff, shared that her own rent had risen far beyond any district raise, and expressed strong support for workforce housing as a relief for dedicated long-term employees.
Lupe Guzman (1m 35s) — As principal of Hoover Community School, she emphasized that all school staff are vital, described how staff cannot afford to live locally, and expressed support for the housing project as a way to rebuild community and reduce staff stress.
Yolanda Richardson (27s) — As a South San Francisco school board visitor, she asked about the quality and durability of the Jefferson housing construction and how rent revenues are handled relative to the bond and general fund.
Janet Coches (1m 5s) — As a parent, she shared that her child lost a beloved fifth-grade teacher who left due to rent affordability, described ongoing transportation challenges for students, and asked for support in keeping teachers in the community.
Erin Washburn (2m 5s) — As president of the Redwood City Teachers Association, she confirmed that teachers genuinely want this program, shared personal examples of teachers burdened by housing costs, and expressed strong support for moving the project forward.

La Junta realizó una sesión cerrada para negociar el precio y los términos de un acuerdo de intercambio de propiedades en 750 Bradford St con The Sobrato Organization, y aprobó varios artículos de consentimiento del programa de bonos, incluyendo una enmienda de gestión de construcción para la Escuela Comunitaria Garfield y mejoras de riego para la Escuela Comunitaria Taft. La Junta aprobó varias mejoras de instalaciones del Programa de Bonos, incluyendo mesas y bancas para Henry Ford School, reemplazo de gabinetes y fregadero para Kennedy Middle School, y Mesas STEAM Rover para Kennedy Middle School. La Junta también aprobó acuerdos de aprendizaje de verano con St. Francis Center, Peninsula Covenant Church e Olimpico Learning, adoptó segundas lecturas de varias políticas incluyendo Política 3250 (Cuotas de Transporte), Política 5142 (Seguridad), y otras, y aprobó el Plan de Gastos Prop 28 y la Exención CBEST para Maestros Sustitutos para 2023-2024.

MAY 10 2023
Reunión de la Junta39m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)Actualizaciones de Políticas

La Junta discutió las negociaciones de RCAA en sesión cerrada, aprobó varios cambios de orden de construcción de la Escuela Comunitaria Garfield y artículos del programa de bonos, e hizo primeras lecturas de seis políticas del distrito incluyendo la Política 3250 (Cuotas de Transporte), la Política 5142 (Seguridad), y la Política 5142.2 (Programa de Rutas Seguras a la Escuela). La Junta también adoptó cuatro resoluciones del Programa de Aire Saludable, Plomería y Eficiencia de Escuelas de California para la Escuela Comunitaria Connect, KIPP Excelencia, Rocketship Redwood City, y el Distrito.

Reunión de la Junta3h 23m

Actualizaciones de Políticas

La Junta tuvo una sesión cerrada para negociar los términos del intercambio de propiedad en 750 Bradford St con The Sobrato Organization, y aprobó múltiples enmiendas de contratos con socios comunitarios incluyendo PlayThrive, WorkEd Inc. y el YMCA of Silicon Valley, junto con la adopción de Materiales de Currículo de Matemáticas para los grados K-8 y el Plan de Servicios de Transporte.

11 puntos de agenda
801:33:34 Comentario públicoOral Communication
Comentarios públicos (1):
Chris Robel (13m 40s) — Presented concerns about lack of transparency in bond spending decisions made by a superintendent subcommittee, Prop 39 compliance issues including administrator salaries charged to bond funds, and vacancies on the Citizens Bond Oversight Committee required by law; called for open subcommittee meetings, clarification of who ensures Prop 39 compliance, and development of formal CBOC appointment procedures.
9.100:03:13 Presentación DiscusiónInformation on Financial Audit Report for RCSD for 2021-2022 School Year
Comentarios públicos (1):
Chris Robel (58s) — Argued that charging administrator salaries to Prop 39 bond funds is a violation of the California Constitution, which explicitly prohibits using bond money for school operating expenses including salaries.

MAR 8 2023
Reunión de la Junta2h 31m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta aprobó varios proyectos del Programa de Bonos Measure T, incluyendo servicios de arquitectura para la Escuela Alternativa Orion y cambios de construcción en la Escuela Comunitaria Garfield, escuchó presentaciones de logros estudiantiles del Instituto de Tecnología McKinley y la Escuela Secundaria Kennedy, y discutió la adopción del currículo de matemáticas para los grados K-5 del año escolar 2023-24. La Junta también adoptó resoluciones para reducir servicios certificados y clasificados en el año fiscal 2022-2023 y aprobó asignaciones de fondos de Prop 28 para el próximo año escolar.

16 puntos de agenda
800:01:52 Comentario públicoOral Communication
Comentarios públicos (1):
Chris Robel (2m 52s) — Raised concerns about chronic absenteeism and poor academic performance at Taft and Garfield schools, criticized the board for not taking immediate action to address student needs, and questioned bond oversight meeting practices.

FEB 15 2023
Reunión de la Junta3h 19m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta adoptó la Resolución No. 24 autorizando la emisión y venta de Bonos de Obligación General, Serie 2023 A, y la Resolución No. 25 determinando el orden de empleo para empleados certificados enfrentando posibles reducciones de servicios. La Junta también aprobó acuerdos con Play Thrive y WorkEd para el año fiscal 2022-2023, revisó reportes financieros e informes de auditoría de escuelas charter incluyendo KIPP Excelencia Community Prep, Connect Community Charter School, y Rocketship Redwood City Charter School, y discutió el panorama de financiamiento del Programa de Bonos Measure T y la lista de artículos bajo $20,000.

14 puntos de agenda

Comentarios públicos (1):
Chris Robel (3m 10s) — Expressed concern about unclear roles and responsibilities for bond oversight, questioning who is reviewing expenditures for Prop 39 compliance and asking about the purpose and reports of an apparent bond subcommittee consisting of Mike Wells, Janet Lawson, and district/contractor staff.

FEB 8 2023
Reunión de la Junta2h 29m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)Actualizaciones de Políticas

La junta aprobó varios artículos del Programa de Bonos Measure T, incluyendo una Orden de Cambio #9 para el trabajo de modernización de la Escuela Alternativa Orion con Duran & Venables, y discutió planes de logros escolares para las escuelas Adelante Selby y Orion. La junta también realizó primeras lecturas de tres políticas que cubren beneficios de salud y bienestar, ausencias estudiantiles y servicios de orientación y consejería.

9 puntos de agenda
500:04:25 Comentario públicoOral Communication
Comentarios públicos (29):
Peter Cohen (1m 22s) — Thanked district staff for correcting enrollment issues with school-of-choice programs, and advocated for expanding Mandarin Immersion TK programs to align with California's 2030 bilingual goal and upcoming Universal TK expansion.
Alexis Betts (38s) — Advocated for expanding MI enrollment to accommodate all interested families, noting the current interest level represents an opportunity for a sustainable, healthy program through 8th grade.
Megan O'Reilly (1m 6s) — Advocated for underserved families in the MI program who need more support, requested a different structural approach to serve both program goals, and urged the district to apply for the TCLP program.
William Dennis (33s) — Expressed strong support for sibling priority in MI enrollment so his younger daughter can attend the same school as his son.
Deepika Germany (spouse speaking) (42s) — Advocated for an additional Mandarin Immersion TK class, stating their daughter is currently on the waitlist and an extra year in the program would be very beneficial.
Tina Chung (36s) — Advocated for additional Mandarin Immersion TK classes to benefit more students in the community, including her son who has some Mandarin background.
Ronnie Le (1m 10s) — Advocated for expanding the Mandarin Immersion program, citing its academic rigor comparable to private immersion schools and the cognitive benefits of bilingual education for her incoming kindergartener.
Jackie Chen (1m 30s) — Shared the emotional difficulty of the MI waitlist experience and urged the board to create two TK MI classes and three kindergarten MI classes.
Julia Selesnick (1m 23s) — Praised the MI program's academic rigor and advocated for expanding both the kindergarten and TK programs to make this high-quality public school option accessible to more children.
Alex (last name not given) (1m 3s) — Advocated for expanding the Mandarin Immersion program at Orion, drawing on personal experience growing up bilingual and emphasizing the broad human and community benefits of early language immersion.
Angela Kang (25s) — Advocated for additional MI TK classes so that all families who want Mandarin Immersion education for their children, including herself with a waitlisted child, can have the opportunity.
Sam Christensen (24s) — Stated that the MI program is the only program his family is interested in, otherwise they will consider private school, and expressed support for expanding TK Mandarin Immersion.
Chris Terry (1m 24s) — Raised concerns about fairness in the MI lottery, particularly the Odyssey Preschool preference (costing $1,700/month) and the TK preference potentially crowding out other kindergarten applicants, and asked for lottery reform or more classes.
Jasper Chen (52s) — Expressed gratitude for the community's transformation and urged the board to invest boldly in expanding the MI program, leveraging the new campus to build a larger, thriving community.
Anna Lee Moraz (1m 39s) — Spoke in Spanish to advocate for more support and accessibility for low-income Latino families in the Mandarin Immersion program, including concerns about the cost of after-school programs.
Keith Simmons (1m 4s) — Asked the board to extend sibling priority to TK, expand MI to two TK classes and three kindergarten classes, and consider expanding Adelante as well given the importance of early language acquisition.
Heather Stamper (56s) — Supported expanding TK and K MI classrooms, noting the program shrank from two kindergarten classes during COVID and expressing concern about the snowball effect of attrition on the program's long-term viability.
Mele Rosallo (1m 6s) — Advocated for more funding and support for MI students with learning disabilities, including a Mandarin resource teacher and aides for math, and an additional TK class.
Richard Pont (43s) — Shared his family's positive experience with the MI program and urged expansion so more families, including those with IEP students, can join and thrive.
Micah Bonara (42s) — Reported his twin boys got off the waitlist and into kindergarten, and called for expansion of both TK and K MI to spare other families the emotional stress of the waitlist process.
Sarah Rutherford (37s) — Asked for consistency in the sibling priority policy between kindergarten and TK, especially given the upcoming rollout of Universal TK.
Tiffany Chao Bev (1m 6s) — Shared that her family moved to Redwood City specifically for the MI program and urged the board to expand kindergarten, TK, and the elementary program, as they would consider relocating if their son cannot get in.
Xiang Yen (39s) — Asked what parents and the community can do to help advance expansion of the MI program, and requested guidance from the board on next steps.
Julia Feng (1m 12s) — Questioned whether it makes sense to artificially cap demand for MI in a district with declining enrollment, and emphasized that TK/Kinder are the only entry windows for immersion programs.
Doshi (last name not given) (1m 4s) — Advocated for an additional MI class, noting that the preschool and sibling preferences effectively eliminated her single-child family's chances despite being a district resident and public school advocate.
Philip Guy (1m 36s) — Urged the board to expand the MI program in every way possible and asked the community to be told how they can help make that happen.
Olanes Villanueva (interpreted) (1m 22s) — Advocated on behalf of Latino families in the MI program for more support, including in-school tutoring services, so that their children are not invisible and can reach grade level and succeed in the program.
Iliana Harrison (37s) — Thanked Dr. Baker for supporting the MI program at the Lunar New Year fair and asked the board for two TK classes and three kindergarten classes, offering parent community support to make it happen.
Kyle Zipes (53s) — Argued that deciding a child's education by lottery is unacceptable and urged the board to accommodate all families who want to attend MI, including investigating a dedicated campus if needed.

La Junta tuvo una sesión cerrada para discutir negociaciones de RCTA y aprobó varios proyectos del programa de bonos, incluyendo mesas de robótica móvil para Kennedy Middle School y un cambio de orden para trabajos en Orion Alternative School. La Junta también adoptó la Resolución 22 certificando la Elección de Bonos de Obligación General de noviembre 2022 y revisó propuestas preliminares de espacio para tres escuelas charter.

DIC 14 2022
Reunión de la Junta2h 21m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda

La Junta aprobó varios proyectos del Programa de Bonos Measure T, incluyendo mesas STEAM para la Escuela de Inmersión en Español Adelante Selby, mejoras de seguridad en todos los sitios escolares, y cámaras adicionales para el Instituto Tecnológico McKinley, mientras que también adoptó la Resolución 21 para el Kindergarten de Día Extendido y aprobó el Reporte del Presupuesto Provisional 2022-23.

15 puntos de agenda
4.2Vice-President
4.4Secretary (Superintendent of Schools)
4.5Trustee Representative to San Mateo County Committee on School District Organization
4.6Legislative Network Member
500:13:14 PresentaciónReception
Recepción
Comentarios públicos (7):
Dennis McBride (3m 1s) — Paid tribute to María Díaz-Slocum's 21 years of service, highlighting her dedication to students, community presence on the Bayside, and personal qualities as a colleague.
Ninfa and Secondino (Familia Unidas community) (2m 8s) — On behalf of Familia Unidas parents and students, thanked María for her incredible support over the years and her contributions to literacy and learning in the community.
Giselle Hill (1m 34s) — As a recently retired city official, praised María as a giant in Redwood City whose equity work predated broad discussion of equity, and welcomed David Weekley and congratulated Cecilia Márquez on continuing to serve.
Michelle Torres (1m 43s) — Thanked María for supporting Familia Unidas and described how the program helped her feel welcomed, supported her family's educational goals, and improved her son's academic performance.
Beatrice Fernanda Senda (2m 4s) — An 11-year-old Familia Unidas participant thanked María and described how the program taught her reading, math, technology, health, and science skills since preschool.
Laura Ramirez (1m 50s) — Thanked María for supporting Familia Unidas and shared how the program helped her family navigate the educational system, adopt healthy habits, and start school successfully.
María Díaz-Slocum (9m 47s) — Gave her farewell remarks, thanking her family, students, staff, community partners, Dr. Baker, and board colleagues for their support during her over two decades of service dedicated to ensuring all children receive the best education possible.
5.1Recognition of María Díaz-Slocum

NOV 16 2022
Reunión de la Junta1h 14m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Presupuesto y Alineación de RecursosBonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)Actualizaciones de Políticas

La Junta discutió las primeras lecturas de varias políticas de personal y empleo incluyendo la Política de la Junta 4030 (No discriminación en el empleo), 4118 y 4218 (Despido/Suspensión/Acción Disciplinaria), y 4216 (Estado Probatorio/Permanente), y aprobó la propuesta inicial de contrato de la Junta para negociaciones con la Asociación de Maestros de Redwood City para 2022-26. La Junta también aprobó artículos del Programa de Bonos Measure T incluyendo movimientos presupuestarios a la Escuela McKinley, Escuela Roosevelt, y proyectos de TI, y la aprobación de puertas de pánico y cercas de estacionamiento de bicicletas para la Escuela Secundaria Kennedy.

La Junta aprobó varios proyectos del Programa de Bonos Measure T, incluyendo mejoras de teatro en McKinley Institute of Technology, cercas y sistemas de seguridad en varias escuelas, e infraestructura de red en Garfield Community School y Orion Alternative School. La Junta también adoptó la Resolución 15 autorizando reuniones electrónicas durante emergencias y la Resolución 16 aprobando opciones de asignaciones de enseñanza del Código de Educación.

14.100:11:59 Presentación Discusión Comentario público Voto (5-0)Adoption of Resolution 2, Ordering a School Bond Election on November 8, 2022, Requesting Consolidation with other Measures Occurring on November 8, 2022, and Authorizing Necessary Actions in Connection TherewithAction
Comentarios públicos (6):
Chris Robel (4m 8s) — Opposed the bond measure, arguing it was a bad time for new taxes, that a parcel tax would be more equitable and flexible, and raising concerns about enrollment decline, interest costs to taxpayers, and the measure's inability to fund teachers or academic programs.
Katerina Schwaidenbeck (2m 3s) — Supported the bond, describing poor bathroom and kitchen facilities at her daughter's school and how a central kitchen could improve the quality and nutritional value of school meals.
Jessica Shade (2m 0s) — Supported the bond, citing the need for updated health, safety, and educational facilities, the potential for a central kitchen for healthier meals, and praising the district's track record of completing Measure T on time and under budget.
Jessica Meunier (1m 46s) — Supported the bond, expressing excitement about the central kitchen potential for improved nutrition, updated safety standards, and the district's track record of fiscal responsibility.
Elmer Martinez (1m 0s) — Supported the bond as a community volunteer, emphasizing the need for critical facility upgrades at aging schools to meet health, safety, and educational standards and strengthen community property values.
Ileana Harrison (2m 19s) — Supported the bond as a pediatrician and parent, praising the new Orion building, noting broad parent support, and advocating especially for a central kitchen to enable cooking fresh meals for students.
Resolution Calling Bond Election - Redwood City SD - November 2022.pdf (167 KB)

JUN 15 2022
Reunión de la Junta3h 29m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta tuvo una sesión cerrada para negociar términos de intercambio de propiedad en 750 Bradford St con The Sobrato Organization, discutió los próximos pasos del programa de Bonos de Obligación General, revisó opciones sobre North Star Academy, y aprobó varios cambios relacionados con bonos y acuerdos incluyendo servicios de comidas para Connect Community Charter School y KIPP Excelencia Community Prep Charter School, servicios de salud mental a través de Effective School Solutions, LLC, y varias actualizaciones de políticas de la Junta.

15 puntos de agenda
9.100:07:53 Presentación Discusión Comentario públicoPresentation and Discussion on the General Obligation (GO) Bond Next StepsDiscussion
Comentarios públicos (2):
Jessica Shade (1m 0s) — Voiced support for the new school bond, called for central kitchen language to be included, and praised the district's efforts.
Katerina Schoenbach (1m 8s) — Speaker was called but unable to unmute and did not provide comment.
RedwoodCitySD1 Bond Measure Board Presentation 6-15-22 2.pdf (3,894 KB)
9.201:06:18 Presentación Discusión Comentario públicoRecommended Options to the School Board Regarding North Star Academy (NSA)Discussion
Comentarios públicos (32):
Matt Schwal (1m 34s) — Argued the NSA Forward Committee was negatively framed and urged the board to reframe the discussion positively, celebrating North Star's success rather than proposing changes.
Cameron Hoffman (5m 10s) — Argued the proposals are being rushed and lack evidence, compared North Star favorably to U46 in Illinois, and proposed a district-wide talent development program called 'SHINE' to serve younger learners rather than restructuring North Star.
Tony Webel (1m 44s) — Shared his experience as a committee member and parent of students at both Roosevelt and North Star, and argued that board bylaws require keeping student learning as the primary focus, finding no data justifying grade configuration or location changes.
Diana Fu (1m 53s) — Drew on her experience helping start the MI program to argue the district should provide more choices and improve other schools rather than restricting a choice program like North Star.
Allie Gilmore (39s) — Speaker was called but did not respond or unmute.
Wenjing Sheng (40s) — Speaker was called but was unable to unmute and did not provide comment.
Lone Hin (1m 4s) — Described her first-grader's unmet academic needs at their neighborhood school and argued that scaling back North Star would leave more students without appropriate challenge.
Megan O'Neill (1m 59s) — Argued that options A through C would serve fewer students and contradict the stated goal of serving more students more effectively, and that neighborhood schools must first be ready to serve gifted learners before any North Star grades are cut.
Matt Collier (48s) — As a parent of young children and former magnet school student himself, urged the board to preserve K–8 North Star as a standalone school and to consider Ms. Hoffman's alternative proposal.
Anthony Low (1m 6s) — Stated that three of the four options effectively close half of North Star, called all options deficient, and said his family would likely leave the district if a 6–8 option were chosen.
Andrew Hoffman (1m 38s) — Argued the recommendations target North Star rather than addressing underperforming schools, warned the options would negatively impact North Star's mission, and urged the board not to adopt incomplete proposals.
Heidi Wall (1m 50s) — Criticized the proposals as short-sighted and premature, cited district-wide problems like declining test scores and teacher retention, and called for using North Star's proven methods to improve other schools rather than dismantling it.
Susan Bouchard (2m 14s) — Argued that capping the number of qualified students allowed to transfer to NSA from neighborhood schools would increase the perception of elitism and create more divisiveness, and asked the board to reject and rework all proposals.
Costas Dimitropoulos (1m 54s) — Asked for empirical evidence supporting any proposed changes and questioned whether there is data proving that altering a high-achieving school like North Star would improve outcomes at lower-performing schools.
Kelly McCarthy (1m 29s) — Questioned whether the community pulse had been taken since COVID, called the effort a waste of resources given widespread learning loss, and urged the board to instead use resources to improve neighborhood schools.
Casey Wright (1m 39s) — Called several options 'casually cruel,' warned the board against falling for the sunk cost fallacy, and implored them to postpone a decision and demand better-researched options.
Catherine Stewart (1m 44s) — Observed that virtually every prior speaker opposed changing or dismantling North Star and argued that the general sentiment—'if it ain't broke, don't fix it'—should guide the board.
Gautam Dutta (1m 17s) — As a local attorney, Asian American civic leader, and North Star parent, echoed 'if it ain't broke, don't fix it,' and asked the board to reject all four options and instead improve support and resources for all schools.
Jing Zhu (1m 26s) — Warned that the proposed changes would create many unhappy parents who become reliable voters against the board, and urged the board to think carefully about the disruption these options would cause to North Star families' daily lives.
Kirsten McMullen (1m 39s) — Called the recommendations insufficient and lacking an evidence-based approach, noting they mainly solve the third-grade attrition issue while creating new problems, and urged deeper research and cost-benefit analysis.
Bara Altaba (1m 25s) — As a parent of current NSA students who transferred from private school because of North Star, urged the board not to break a nationally recognized school and to instead bring other schools up to North Star's level.
Michelle Fogarty (1m 31s) — Argued the proposals take direct aim at one successful school without solving district-wide shortcomings, criticized use of outdated data from 2018, and urged the board to conduct new research before implementing any changes.
Katie Goetz (50s) — As a North Star Forward Committee member, encouraged the board to consider and move forward with the proposal to make North Star a 6–8 school on the Bayside, stating it best addresses the committee's three charges.
Christopher Bouchard (1m 36s) — Asked the board to reject all four proposals and pause major changes, citing a flawed and biased committee process built on four-year-old data, and called for a reset with current community input.
David Wright (1m 20s) — Implored the board to defer the decision and request a revised set of options aligned to the committee's stated goals, warning against committing to a process not in students' best interests.
Mike Beebe (1m 44s) — Summarized three broadly supported elements from Dr. Baker's plans (outreach, test prep access, logistics like transportation), suggested spreading entrance grades across third through fifth to reduce the third-grade attrition pinch, and emphasized district-wide learning loss as the true priority.
Meredith Park (1m 34s) — Raised traffic concerns near North Star, warned against adding K–2 at the Duane location, and suggested exploring a neighborhood boundary middle school with an IB program on Duane as an alternative approach.
Nathan Schmidt (38s) — Using a 'ten boats' analogy, argued the board's duty is to save the underperforming schools rather than compromising the one that is succeeding.
Mohamed Tabrizi (1m 17s) — Ceded time to Cameron Hoffman and added that spending district resources on this process instead of lower-income students' education is unacceptable, urging trustees to reject all options and use scientific data.
Lily Melton (50s) — As an Orion parent, urged the board to move forward with bold structural changes and noted that a large community of parents who want district-wide improvement supports significant change even if not loudly represented tonight.
Beatrice Chan (1m 18s) — Called the options deeply flawed, asked the board to instead focus on introducing enrichment programs in community schools and helping current students meet grade-level expectations rather than dismantling North Star.
Maribel Ng (1m 41s) — Shared her child's struggles before finding North Star, emphasized that students who go there have often struggled elsewhere and need an environment that understands them, and suggested spreading that philosophy across all Redwood City schools.
NSA Forward Committee Feedback. Names Redacted.pdf (524 KB)North Star Forward_ Board Recommendation (1).pdf (503 KB)

JUN 13 2022
Reunión de la Junta
📄 Actas↗ Agenda

La Junta realizó una sesión cerrada para hacer una evaluación del desempeño del Superintendente conforme a la Sección 54957 del Código de Educación.

Año Escolar 2021–22

34 reuniones del archivo de BoardDocs.

JUN 1 2022
Reunión de la Junta1h 3m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La junta aprobó varios proyectos financiados por bonos, incluyendo un proyecto de estacionamiento trasero en la Escuela Comunitaria Garfield con Galeb Paving, Mesas STEAM para la Escuela Comunitaria Hoover con RDI, y mejoras eléctricas de HVAC en la Escuela Comunitaria Garfield con Atlas Pellizzari. La junta también recibió actualizaciones sobre la adopción de materiales de historia-ciencias sociales de la escuela intermedia y apoyo instructivo para maestros de grados primarios, y adoptó la Resolución 36 para autorizar reuniones electrónicas de la junta durante emergencias.

5 puntos de agenda

MAY 25 2022
Reunión de la Junta1h 40m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta aprobó varios cambios de órdenes del Programa de Bonos Measure T para proyectos de construcción en Garfield Community School, Orion Alternative School y Taft Community School, y escuchó una actualización de la Universidad Stanford sobre la investigación de inmersión bilingüe en Adelante Selby Spanish Immersion School. La Junta también sostuvo sesiones cerradas para discutir litigio anticipado y negociaciones de CSEA, e hizo primeras lecturas de cinco políticas y normas de la junta incluyendo políticas sobre organizaciones conectadas a escuelas, empleo fuera de la escuela, paros laborales, y educación para niños sin hogar.

15 puntos de agenda
800:03:29 Comentario públicoOral Communication
Comentarios públicos (1):
Maria Stockton (3m 38s) — Representing CSEA, she expressed concern about repeated requests to change the school calendar (winter break) to align with Sequoia High School, arguing it creates hardship for classified employees and child care families, and asked the board to review the documentation and rationale before agreeing to the change.

ABR 27 2022
Reunión de la Junta1h 20m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta aprobó varios proyectos del Programa de Bonos Measure T, incluyendo trabajos de paisajismo y estabilización de terrenos en varias escuelas, instalación de cargadores de vehículos eléctricos en la Escuela Alternativa Orion y la Escuela Comunitaria Taft, y órdenes de cambio para proyectos de modernización en la Escuela Comunitaria Garfield y la Escuela Comunitaria Taft. La Junta también aprobó nuevas políticas de la junta sobre preparación para emergencias, no discriminación en el empleo y acoso sexual, junto con acciones de personal y la Subvención del Programa de Oportunidades de Aprendizaje Ampliado.

ABR 20 2022
Special Meeting2h 15m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda

La Junta revisó las ideas preliminares generadas por el Comité North Star Forward en esta reunión especial.

2 puntos de agenda
600:07:41 Presentación Discusión Comentario públicoDiscussion Items
Comentarios públicos (19):
David Weekley (31s) — Urged the board to center students and maximize every student's potential, changing North Star if it harms non-North Star students.
Lily Milton (2m 25s) — Representing committee members, she thanked the board for the process and recommended changing grade configuration and bringing compacted/enrichment curriculum to all schools, while urging urgent action on equity and broader structural issues.
Unidentified speaker (2m 7s) — Argued that Bayside access data is misleading since many Bayside families already access North Star via choice/charter, that North Star is not broken and serves its students well at no extra cost, and urged focus on the 70% of students behind academically rather than altering a well-functioning program.
John Kinsel (1m 34s) — Expressed strong support for North Star as a program that keeps students challenged and engaged, and suggested the focus should be on improving other schools rather than changing North Star.
Owen Webel (1m 21s) — As a North Star graduate, described how he was disengaged at his previous school and thrived at North Star where he found peers at his level and learned more effectively.
Tony Webel (2m 1s) — As a parent of multiple children in the district including one at North Star and one at Roosevelt, he emphasized that the primary objective should be the best equitable learning environment for all students, and flagged concerns about the Jamboard document as potentially misleading.
Jessica Nodi (57s) — As a Roy Cloud parent and educator, she expressed support for starting North Star at kindergarten, bringing enrichment programming to all schools, and offering enrollment by equal percentages from each school.
Katie Morello (1m 5s) — As a Henry Ford parent, she expressed concern about the emotional impact on children losing friends when classmates leave for North Star in third grade and suggested middle school might be a better entry point.
Kaylee Vega (1m 19s) — As a Henry Ford parent, she said her family chose the district largely because of North Star access, expressed concern about unmet academic needs at neighborhood schools, and urged the board to improve other schools rather than dismantle a highly ranked program.
Loan Hin (1m 40s) — As a Henry Ford parent and former magnet school student herself, she said her family may leave the district if North Star is not available at third grade, and argued that magnet programs like North Star provide economic mobility through education and that equity issues can be addressed without eliminating the format.
Elle Kolekar (1m 8s) — As a Bayside family with children at both Hillside and North Star schools, she argued that inequity is a deeper district-wide issue that North Star alone did not create, and called on the board to invest in vibrant, diverse Bayside community schools.
Alice Harkey (1m 55s) — As North Star's parent club president, she credited North Star with transforming her children's education, supported equal access to testing and more Bayside outreach, busing, and after-school support, and asked the board to preserve the curriculum and enrichment opportunities.
Shonda Stewart (1m 37s) — As a parent with children at both North Star and Orion, she raised concerns about North Star's fundraising concentrating resources inequitably, the school's culture being unwelcoming to lower-income families, and the limited Bayside outreach compared to Mandarin immersion community outreach.
Katie Goetz (1m 14s) — As a North Star Forward Committee member, she argued that the district has an obligation to make enriched programming available to all students, not just those identified through testing as gifted, and thanked the board for its work toward greater equity.
Nicole Kowaki (1m 10s) — Speaking on behalf of 20 co-signing district parents, she cited stark demographic disparities between North Star and the broader district and called publicly funded North Star as currently structured unethical, urging the board to support the equitable solutions proposed by the committee.
Daryl Norcott (1m 33s) — As a Clifford parent, he argued that North Star reinforces achievement gaps through biased standardized testing, racially segregates school communities, makes children who don't qualify feel defective, and divides communities, and urged bringing enrichment methods to students rather than concentrating them in one school.
Heidi von Brielle (1m 40s) — She raised environmental concerns about car trips to North Star, argued that all Redwood City schools share the same finish line, and called for implementing options that eliminate the 'musical chairs' caused by third-grade migration.
Mike Beebe (1m 40s) — As a North Star third-grade parent, he urged careful evaluation of whether the issue is admissions process design or information and logistics barriers before switching to a hard percentage admit model, and suggested expanded information and shuttle service for Bayside families.
Ann Hinesek (1m 39s) — As a North Star Forward Committee member, she highlighted bringing the Renzulli method to all schools and equalizing PTO/PTA funding distribution as key equity ideas, and supported changing grade configuration, hiring Spanish-speaking staff, capping per-school admittance, and possibly moving North Star to the Bayside.
6.1Review of Preliminary Ideas Generated by North Star Forward CommitteeDiscussion
North Star Forward 2.12.20.pdf (85 KB)North Star Forward 2.25.20.pdf (1,011 KB)North Star Forward 3.9.20.pdf (110 KB)North Star Forward Committee 1.20.22.pdf (789 KB)NSA Data 2022.pdf (1,216 KB)NSA Forward 2.10.22.pdf (1,245 KB)NSA Forward 3.24.22.pdf (170 KB)NSA Forward 4.07.2022.pdf (180 KB)NSA Forward Jamboard April 7, 2022.ALL.pdf (4,928 KB)NSA Forward PPT 3.3.22.pdf (175 KB)NSA Forward Survey 3.2022 with personal reference comments redacted.pdf (562 KB)NSA Forward_ Preliminary Ideas for Board Discussion.pdf (1,337 KB)Report on Interviews with Bayside Parents.pdf (59 KB)North Star Forward_ Board Study Session.pdf (930 KB)
ABR 13 2022
Reunión de la Junta1h 23m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)Actualizaciones de Políticas

La Junta aprobó elementos de consentimiento del Programa de Bonos Measure T, incluyendo una lista de seguimiento para proyectos menores a $20,000 y una enmienda de acuerdo de inspector para Orion Alternative y Taft Community School. La Junta también realizó primeras lecturas de varias actualizaciones de políticas sobre preparación ante emergencias, no discriminación en el empleo, procedimientos disciplinarios de empleados y acoso sexual, mientras aprobaba la extensión del acuerdo de servicio de alimentos con Sodexo America, LLC para el año fiscal 2022-23 y la adopción de Materiales de Artes del Lenguaje de Inmersión en Mandarín.

14 puntos de agenda
500:07:22 Comentario públicoOral Communication
Comentarios públicos (1):
Sue Jorgensen (3m 15s) — Expressed concern about the lack of communication following the board's vote to close the district librarian position, recounted the history of library administration changes over 15 years, and urged the board to provide continuity by establishing a district librarian position.

MAR 23 2022
Reunión de la Junta1h 48m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta revisó los resultados de la Encuesta de Viabilidad de Medida de Bonos 2022 y recibió una actualización del Programa de Bonos, mientras aprobaba varios elementos de consentimiento relacionados con bonos incluyendo una propuesta de superficie de caucho para la estructura de juegos en la Escuela Garfield y un cambio de orden de proyecto con Rodan Builders. La Junta también aprobó el Reporte de Presupuesto Interino Segundo 2021-22 y un acuerdo con la Liga de Actividades del Alguacil del Condado de San Mateo para ofrecer programas extracurriculares en las escuelas Garfield, Clifford y Hoover.

MAR 9 2022
Reunión de la Junta1h 39m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta aprobó varios proyectos del Programa de Bonos Measure T, incluyendo trabajo de concreto y asfalto, un aula al aire libre y mejoras de equipo de cocina en la Escuela Comunitaria Hoover, además de refuerzo estructural en el sitio 3150 Granger Way, mientras adoptaba resoluciones sobre reducciones de servicios certificados y clasificados para el año fiscal 2021-2022 y discutía cambiar las políticas de mascarillas de obligatorias a fuertemente recomendadas en las escuelas.

13 puntos de agenda
800:05:20 Comentario públicoOral Communication
Comentarios públicos (5):
Eric (technology staff) (3m 33s) — Read a letter on behalf of the district technology team describing how classified tech staff were required to work on-site throughout the pandemic without receiving the same financial compensation as teachers who were later incentivized to return.
Ryan Sue (3m 18s) — Followed up on the letter, questioning why the tech department and other classified staff who worked on-site the entire pandemic were not offered the same financial incentives as teachers, and expressing disappointment that the proposed MOU compensation of five restricted holidays was not comparable to the teacher stipend.
Maria Stockton (2m 23s) — A 43-year district employee described a decline in respect for classified staff, noted inequitable treatment compared to certificated staff during COVID, and invited board members to visit schools and speak directly with classified employees.
Ana Solorio (2m 19s) — Presented a letter with signatures from classified members requesting board support in MOU negotiations, emphasizing that classified workers who were on-site during the pandemic deserve compensation comparable to the teacher stipends, not just five holiday days.
Johanna Rasmussen (1m 30s) — Described her fourth-grade son being assaulted for the third time at Roosevelt School despite a safety plan in place, and asked the board to intervene and address escalating bullying and violence on the school's playground.
9.1Information on Financial Audit Report for RCSD for 2020-2021 School Year

La Junta también adoptó la Resolución 26 sobre reducción de empleados certificados, aprobó los calendarios de instrucción para 2022-2023, y realizó una segunda lectura de las Políticas de Junta 5116.1 (Inscripción Abierta Intradistrital) y 5117 (Asistencia Interdistrital).

12.500:19:54 Presentación Discusión Comentario públicoSecond Reading and Approval of Board Policy 5116.1 - Intradistrict Open EnrollmentAction
Comentarios públicos (18):
David Weekley (5m 25s) — Opposed the policy, arguing that restricting intradistrict transfers hurts students and the district by limiting parental choice, and that the way to protect programs like Mandarin Immersion is to invest in their quality rather than lock families in.
Eric Liu (50s) — Opposed the policy as an Asian American parent of a Mandarin Immersion student, stating that barring choice program students from applying to North Star felt discriminatory and contrary to Redwood City's values of diversity and equality.
Connie Kwan (1m 42s) — Opposed the policy, describing the unfair dilemma it creates for parents who must choose between staying in a choice program or leaving early to preserve eligibility for North Star, and asked the committee to consider alternative solutions to attrition.
Peter Cowan (3m 2s) — Opposed the policy, noting the difficult bind it creates for families with children in different programs, criticized the timing during enrollment season, argued the policy is a band-aid on a third-grade attrition cliff, and called for transparency instead of 'unspoken rules.'
Dennis Lee (2m 9s) — Opposed the policy as a parent of a rising kindergartner accepted to Mandarin Immersion, arguing it raises the stakes of choosing a school by requiring parents to predict their child's needs years in advance and that it unfairly limits future choices while potentially benefiting private school families.
Michelle Hsu (1m 20s) — Opposed the policy, arguing that North Star and Mandarin Immersion are not competing on fair ground given North Star's third-grade entry and testing-based admissions, and urged the committee to address equity issues in North Star's admissions process instead.
Jennifer Barnett (1m 44s) — Opposed the policy as a Mandarin Immersion kindergarten parent, calling it shortsighted and inequitable, arguing it will not fix the exodus to North Star and instead should prompt examination of North Star's own problematic policies.
Diana Chang (1m 27s) — Submitted a written comment opposing the restriction on transferring between non-boundary schools, calling it unnecessarily limiting for families who discover a choice school is not the right fit, and questioning whether there are better ways to distribute opportunities equitably.
Alvin Cheon (2m 35s) — As an Orion Parent Participation parent, expressed concern that removing intradistrict transfer incentives would push families away from public school, and suggested alternatives such as embedding advanced coursework at the classroom level within each school rather than concentrating it at North Star.
Eric Garcia (1m 13s) — Submitted a written comment opposing the policy, arguing it removes parents' ability to choose what is best for their children's evolving educational needs and that schools losing students to transfers should instead be empowered to improve their programs.
Christina Jade Lee Simmons (3m 7s) — Opposed the policy and called for deeper structural reform, arguing that North Star perpetuates inequality as the least economically integrated school in the district, and encouraged the board to consider starting North Star in sixth grade and investing in enrichment at every elementary school.
Kyle Zeibes (2m 38s) — Opposed the policy as a Mandarin Immersion kindergarten parent and budget committee member, arguing it will harm equity by trapping low-income families and push higher-income families to private school, and suggested delaying North Star until sixth grade as a preferable alternative.
Brian Hicks (3m 11s) — Opposed the policy, challenging the administration's framing of it as formalizing an 'unwritten rule' since the actual long-standing practice has been that families can move between choice programs, and requested that any future policy not be applied retroactively to families already enrolled.
Elle Kolekar (2m 13s) — Opposed the policy as an Orion Parent Participation parent, warning it will cause families interested in North Star to attend private school for K–2 and could devastate Orion's Parent Participation program, and instead urged the district to invest in making neighborhood schools more attractive.
Keith Simmons (1m 33s) — Opposed the policy as a Mandarin Immersion parent, echoing others' concerns and specifically warning that the superintendent exception process would create racial inequities because immigrant families face language and cultural barriers to requesting special exceptions.
Ting Cheng Wang (1m 14s) — Opposed the policy, noting that it effectively forces parents to choose their child's middle school at age five, which is an unfair burden given that only non-choice school families retain access to both Kennedy and North Star.
Megan O'Reilly Green (1m 55s) — Expressed nuanced support for pausing the policy and argued that the fundamental problem is North Star's structure and the perception that it is the 'best' school, stating she does not believe the proposed policy would harm Mandarin Immersion recruitment and that the district's real challenge is North Star's incompatibility with its equity mission.
Elizabeth Flavel (2m 11s) — As an active Adelante community member, shared her personal experience that the flexibility to try kindergarten with the option to transfer gave her family the confidence to enroll in Spanish Immersion, and cautioned the board about potential unintended consequences of the policy on incoming family decisions.
BP_AR 5116.1 Intradistrict Open Enrollment.pdf (167 KB)

Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta aprobó varios proyectos del Programa de Bonos Measure T, incluyendo acuerdos de construcción para la Escuela Alternativa Orion (refugio para almuerzo, estructuras de juego, reemplazo de techo), instalación de cargadores eléctricos en la Escuela Comunitaria Garfield y mejoras eléctricas en varios sitios. La Junta también realizó primeras lecturas de tres políticas de asistencia y matriculación (Políticas de la Junta 3311, 5117 y 5116.1) y adoptó la Resolución 25 autorizando reuniones electrónicas durante emergencias.

Comentarios públicos (3):
Pam Barrage (2m 49s) — Expressed concern that staff were not receiving adequate KN95/N95 masks and requested that teachers receive five masks per week through the end of the surge at minimum, without having to pay out of pocket.
Ayla Spinelli Stanc (2m 10s) — Asked about expediting the volunteer clearance process so that Healthy Cities tutors could be matched with students more quickly, and offered to help with volunteer paperwork.
Rebecca Fouch (2m 50s) — Asked whether a student whose day-five isolation end date falls on a Monday could use a home test result to return to school, and what the protocol is for school-administered testing for positive students.

10.100:56:39 Presentación Discusión Comentario público Voto (approved)Revisiting the School Board's March 11, 2020 Vote on Interdistrict TransfersAction
Comentarios públicos (2):
Jen Parsons (37s) — Read by staff: Expressed support for grandfathering Mandarin Immersion program siblings because families had been encouraged to join the program and honoring that commitment builds trust with the school community.
Lily Milton (1m 12s) — As co-PTO president at Orion, expressed support for retaining the Mandarin Immersion program siblings, citing the difficulty of recovering from family attrition and emphasizing a community-first approach.

Comentarios públicos (2):
Allison Olford (1m 34s) — Thanked the board and staff for addressing parent concerns, asked the committee not to merely focus on optics but to have the district take ownership of concrete goals, and recommended bringing in a paid expert in gifted education equity to guide the committee.
Matthew Schwal (1m 59s) — Recommended using the Form 8 Cogat as a universal screener and then having qualifying students take the Form 9 as a confirmation assessment, arguing this approach aligns with the test-maker's intent and would generate useful comparative data for the North Star Forward Committee.

Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta discutió la Visión y Planes del Departamento de Tecnología para 2021-22, revisó artículos del Programa de Bonos Measure T incluyendo cambios de orden para la Escuela Taft y la Escuela Garfield, y adoptó la Resolución 13 apoyando la orden ejecutiva del Gobernador Newsom requiriendo vacunación contra el Covid-19 para estudiantes. La Junta también aprobó el Plan de Gastos ESSER III y realizó segundas lecturas de varias políticas incluyendo la Política 5132 sobre Vestimenta y Apariencia y la Política 6020 sobre Participación de Padres.

Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta aprobó varios proyectos de mejora de instalaciones, incluyendo sistemas de cámaras de seguridad para las escuelas Garfield, Orion Alternative y Taft, reemplazos de ventanas en Kennedy Middle School, y otras mejoras de sitios financiadas por bonos. La Junta también discutió y adoptó la Resolución 9 instando al Gobernador Newsom a requerir vacunación contra Covid-19 para estudiantes, y realizó primeras lecturas de varias actualizaciones de pólizas incluyendo código de vestimenta (Póliza 5132) e involucramiento de padres (Póliza 6020).

SEP 1 2021
Reunión de la Junta1h 44m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Actualizaciones de Políticas

La Junta discutió el año escolar 2021-22 y presentó tres nuevas políticas: Política 1313 sobre civismo, Política 5145.9 sobre conducta motivada por odio, y Política 5141.4 sobre prevención y reporte de abuso infantil. La Junta también aprobó acuerdos de servicios con Star Vista, One Life Counseling y Peninsula Community Center para el año fiscal 2021-22, y adoptó las Resoluciones 5 y 6 relacionadas con la reducción de ciertos servicios clasificados.

▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda

La Junta realizó un retiro para discutir los objetivos del LCAP para el próximo año escolar, revisó los números de inscripción al 26 de agosto de 2021, escuchó presentaciones de reportes escolares para 2021-22, y planificó para las reuniones presenciales de la Junta para 2021-22. La Junta también se reunió en sesión cerrada para tratar asuntos de personal y evaluaciones de desempeño.

Comentarios públicos (2):
Katherine Stewart (2m 41s) — Requested that her son, who will be in independent study at North Star for third grade, have access to grade-level curriculum materials, digital apps such as BrainPop and Level Chinese, and resources equivalent to in-person students so he can seamlessly return to campus once a COVID vaccine for children is available.
Maribel Eng (1m 32s) — Expressed concern that without physical distancing in classrooms, the district may not be maximizing COVID transmission prevention, particularly given the high transmissibility of the Delta variant, and asked whether schools had considered implementing at least some distancing measures indoors.

Año Escolar 2020–21

34 reuniones del archivo de BoardDocs.

MAY 19 2021
Reunión de la Junta3h 19m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta tuvo una sesión cerrada para discutir asuntos de personal y litigios anticipados, luego aprobó varios proyectos del Programa de Bonos Measure T incluyendo trabajos de modernización en Garfield, Roy Cloud, Hoover, Clifford y Kennedy Middle School, junto con un acuerdo de arrendamiento para 3150 Granger Way y un Plan de Subvención de Oportunidades de Aprendizaje Ampliado para 2021-22. La Junta también realizó primeras lecturas de varias actualizaciones de políticas incluyendo Política 0520.1 (Mejora Escolar), Política 5145.3 (No Discriminación/Acoso) y Política 6142.8 (Educación de la Salud Integral), y discutió el plan de reapertura para el año escolar 2021-22.

18 puntos de agenda
800:04:04 Comentario públicoOral Communication
Comentarios públicos (2):
Maria Abundiz (2m 32s) — Congratulated the 350+ classified employees of the district during Classified Employee Appreciation Week and thanked them for their dedication to students and the community.
Maria Stockton (2m 30s) — Praised classified employees during California School Employees Week and challenged each board member to visit every school site and personally meet classified staff when returning to in-person operations.
9.100:10:44 Presentación DiscusiónRecognition of 2020-21 Induction Program Participants
9_1_202021InductionColloquium_0.pdf (6,121 KB)
9.200:36:36 Presentación Discusión Comentario públicoRecognition of Retiree Staff
Comentarios públicos (3):
Kevin Sugar (55s) — Congratulated both the induction program participants and the retirees, and offered a special personal tribute to his wife Lisa Sugar on her retirement.
Carrie Schmidt (51s) — Wished all retirees well and offered special thanks to Lisa Sugar, Jeanette Serrano, Peter Bartok, and Aura Bartok for their impact as colleagues.
Michelle Buetler (1m 15s) — Honored the retiring Hoover staff, especially Lisa Sugar whose mentorship shaped the entire Hoover special education team, and celebrated the legacy of all retiring classified and certificated staff.
9_2_202021Retirees_0.pdf (54 KB)

MAY 5 2021
Reunión de la Junta1h 26m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta sostuvo sesiones cerradas para discutir negociaciones de CSEA y RCTA, revisó proyectos de modernización del programa de bonos Measure T para Clifford School y Garfield Community School, y discutió el plan de reapertura del distrito para el año escolar 2021-22 y el nuevo currículo de ciencias para los grados 6-8.

14 puntos de agenda
800:04:43 Comentario públicoOral Communication
Comentarios públicos (3):
Erin Washburn (3m 15s) — As RCTA president, expressed gratitude to RCSD administration for their remarkable work throughout the pandemic, from distance learning technology to safely reopening campuses.
Indra Hernandez (2m 48s) — Thanked the tech department and board for their support during the pandemic, and advocated for renewal of the Math Shelf program subscription for preschool students.
Jennifer Tanti (1m 29s) — A 7th and 8th grade social studies teacher at Clifford, she expressed gratitude to the board and district administration for exceptional organizational support and resources throughout the pandemic.

MAR 10 2021
Reunión de la Junta3h 33m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta tuvo una sesión cerrada para discutir dos asuntos de personal de empleados y aprobó varios proyectos del Programa de Bonos Measure T, incluyendo honorarios de arquitectos para las Escuelas Garfield y Taft, servicios de seguridad para la Escuela Roosevelt, paisajismo para la Escuela Hoover, cercas para la Escuela Adelante Selby, y cambios en los proyectos de modernización de la Escuela Roy Cloud y la Escuela Taft Community. La Junta también adoptó la Resolución 16 sobre despidos de empleados certificados para el año fiscal 2021-22 y aprobó el Reporte Presupuestario Intermedio 2020-21.

18 puntos de agenda
800:04:47 Comentario públicoOral Communication
Comentarios públicos (2):
Anna Salario (3m 22s) — Speaking on behalf of classified members, she asked the board for compensation comparable to the stipends teachers are receiving, arguing classified employees' responsibilities have increased significantly during the pandemic.
Sue Jorgensen (2m 26s) — Echoed the classified compensation concern from an equity standpoint, noting that classified staff and teachers working side-by-side are receiving very different financial recognition for the same in-person risks.
9.1Scholastic Bloom Hybrid Plant Projects (7th and 8th Grade Science at Clifford School)
9_1_7thGradeScholasticHybridPlantContest_0.pdf (14,496 KB)
9.2Bond Program Update
10.1Approval of Measure T Bond Program Tracking List of Items Under $20KAction
10_1_TrackingListForItemsUnder20K_0.pdf (88 KB)
1200:41:10 Presentación Discusión Comentario públicoDiscussion Items
Comentarios públicos (4):
Pam Barash (2m 48s) — A kindergarten teacher expressed concern that staff who travel over spring break may be required to quarantine upon return despite being vaccinated, and asked whether the same quarantine requirement would apply to students and families, given that schools were reopening only three days after spring break.
Indra Hernandez (3m 21s) — A CDC/preschool staff member raised concerns about inconsistent COVID protocols across sites, the wellness survey being misunderstood, uneven enforcement of Wednesday off-campus rules, the timing of second vaccine doses relative to the return date, and requested COVID testing for students before they enter preschool classrooms.
Stephanie Chen (2m 39s) — A parent asked whether the district could establish more uniform return-to-school formats across all schools for equity reasons, rather than having disparate plans at each site.
Shawna Hackworth (1m 4s) — A parent asked whether students currently placed at a school of choice would lose their placement for the following year if they chose to enroll in the proposed virtual academy for 2021-22.

▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta tuvo sesiones cerradas para discutir negociaciones con CSEA y RCTA, aprobó varios proyectos del Programa de Bonos Measure T incluyendo remodelaciones de baños en kindergarten y reemplazo de asfalto en Hoover Community School, y discutió la respuesta al COVID-19 y los resultados del Examen de Diagnóstico iReady. La Junta también hizo lecturas iniciales de la Política 1340 - Acceso a Registros del Distrito y la Política 3280 - Venta o Arrendamiento de Propiedad.

15 puntos de agenda
9.1Presentation from the Mandarin Immersion Program on their Lunar New Year Celebration
10.1Approval of Kindergarten Classroom Bathrooms Remodel Proposal for Hoover Community School with Beals MartinAction
10_1_BathroomRemodelProposalWithBealsMartin_0.pdf (296 KB)
10.4Approval of Measure T Bond Program Tracking List for Items Under $20KAction (Consent)
10_4_TrackingListForItemsUnder20K_0.pdf (87 KB)
12.100:18:09 Presentación Discusión Comentario públicoDiscussion on COVID-19Discussion
Comentarios públicos (6):
Cameron Hoffman (2m 59s) — Urged the board and administration to reopen schools with urgency and decisiveness, calling out passive language and noting that only Taft met the county's definition of 'reopened,' and requesting an MOU deadline.
Allison Oford (2m 48s) — Requested a regular reopening dashboard for community visibility, thanked staff returning without vaccines, and argued that returning to campus this school year is worthwhile for mental health and to prepare for a stronger fall.
Mary Beth Maloney (52s) — Welcomed the progress toward reopening but urged concrete action for middle school students to return to campus given the crucial developmental period.
Mercedes Kielczkowski (3m 4s) — As a child and adolescent psychiatrist, described worsening mental health impacts of distance learning and implored the board to reopen schools immediately, citing strong local and national safety data.
Katherine Stewart (1m 56s) — Expressed that she would not send her child back until a pediatric COVID vaccine is available, and asked the district to support robust distance learning options for families who choose to remain remote.
Heidi (2m 17s) — Criticized the board's prior lack of guidance for eroding trust between teachers and parents, called for immediate policies to return students safely, and asked for more transparent and proactive communication from the administration.

FEB 12 2021
Retiro1h 44m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda

La Junta realizó un retiro enfocado en la planificación estratégica, incluyendo discusiones en sesión cerrada sobre negociaciones presupuestarias y vivienda para empleados, seguidas de conversaciones abiertas sobre equidad y una revisión completa de la misión, metas y valores del distrito.

FEB 10 2021
Reunión de la Junta2h 58m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta tuvo una sesión cerrada para hablar sobre solicitudes de licencia de empleados y negociaciones laborales con CSEA y RCTA, luego aprobó varios proyectos del Programa de Bonos Measure T incluyendo el diseño de infraestructura de carga para autobuses eléctricos, modernización del campus en la Escuela de Inmersión en Español Adelante, y varias mejoras de instalaciones, mientras también ratificaba un acuerdo tentativo con la Asociación de Empleados de Escuelas de California y revisaba reportes financieros de escuelas autónomas.

21 puntos de agenda
800:07:53 Comentario públicoOral Communication
Comentarios públicos (1):
Sue Jorgensen (3m 31s) — A school librarian urged the board to establish a dedicated budget line item for school libraries, noting that current funding has been inconsistent, insufficient, and in some cases zero, leaving libraries unable to replace lost books or purchase basic supplies.

1202:39:57 Presentación DiscusiónDiscussion Items
Comentarios públicos (3):
Marisol Mendoza (1m 53s) — An instructional assistant at Henry Ford expressed disappointment that the district rejected CSEA's request for extra compensation for classified staff working on-site during the pandemic, arguing that their workload and health risks have increased just as teachers' have.
Michael Hofer (55s) — A Roy Cloud parent asked what the plan B would be if the current vaccine-dependent reopening plan does not materialize, and when parents would know whether children would return in person by April or by August.
Maria Stockton (3m 13s) — A classified staff representative challenged the board to visit school sites and witness the increased workloads of bus drivers, office staff, food service, tech, and instructional assistants, and requested that classified employees be given an opportunity to present to the board about how their jobs have changed during the pandemic.

Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta también aprobó múltiples proyectos del Programa de Bonos Measure T, incluyendo trabajo de construcción y modernización en Orion Alternative School, Clifford School, Roy Cloud School, Roosevelt School, Adelante Selby School, y Hoover School, y tomó acción sobre la Propuesta Inicial de la Junta Directiva para negociaciones 2021-22 con RCTA.

Reunión de la Junta3h 31m
▶ Video📄 Actas📝 Transcripción↗ Agenda
Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta tuvo una sesión cerrada para negociar el precio y los términos de pago del sitio de la Escuela Adelante en 3150 Granger Way, y aprobó numerosas propuestas del programa de bonos para mejoras de instalaciones, incluyendo accesorios de plomería sin contacto, sistemas de seguridad y trabajo de sitio en múltiples escuelas. La Junta también aprobó el Reporte del Presupuesto Provisional 2020-21 y discutió actualizaciones a la Política de la Junta 6158 sobre Estudio Independiente y la Política de la Junta 5141.22 sobre Enfermedades Infecciosas.

Bonos de Instalaciones (Medida S/T)

La Junta aprobó múltiples proyectos de construcción y modernización en las escuelas del distrito como parte del Programa de Bonos Measure T, incluyendo cambios de órdenes para Henry Ford Elementary, Clifford Elementary, Hoover Community School, Kennedy Middle School y otros sitios, además de mejoras en las instalaciones como plomería sin contacto y sistemas de energía de respaldo. La Junta también discutió los impactos del COVID-19, recibió una presentación de apoyo a la salud mental de la Universidad de Stanford, y revisó la Política de la Junta 0415 sobre Equidad para aprobación de segunda lectura.
